talk_metadata.institution_or_pharma: Massachusetts General Hospital and Harvard Medical School
talk_metadata.session_title: The Next Frontier in Minimal Residual Disease: Solid Tumors
talk_metadata.talk_title: From Persistence to Relapse: Evolution of Drug Resistance in Lung Cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "Unfortunately, this was short-lived, and the mutations returned along with a new BRAF V600E mutation."
Speaker said: "We observed increased APOBEC mutational signatures in most treated resistant tumors. And in most cases, these APOBEC mutations were not present before therapy."

Speaker said: "In control cells, we see cells initially respond and then slowly become resistant as the persister clones grow out. However, in APOBEC3A knockdown cells, this persister outgrowth is largely abolished."

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Therapy‑induced APOBEC3A mutagenesis generating new resistance mutations
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: Speaker noted increased APOBEC signatures in resistant tumors but did not explain why some tumors acquire high APOBEC activity while others do not.

key_data_gaps: No clinical trial data linking APOBEC inhibition to patient outcomes
Lack of in‑vivo validation of APOBEC‑targeted strategies

crispro_opportunity.description: CrisPRO could model APOBEC‑driven mutational trajectories to predict emergent resistance mutations and guide combination strategies that pre‑empt APOBEC‑mediated evolution.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted that APOBEC3A‑induced mutagenesis drives late‑emerging resistance mutations in EGFR/ALK‑treated lung cancer.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Use CrisPRO's computational spine to simulate adaptive mutability and identify upstream regulators of APOBEC activation for therapeutic targeting.

cited_competitors.name: Bivona group
cited_competitors.context: reported similar APOBEC3B role in resistance

unresolved_questions: What determines which tumors up‑regulate APOBEC3A versus APOBEC3B during therapy?
Can pharmacologic inhibition of APOBEC enzymes improve clinical outcomes?

talk_metadata.session_title: The Next Frontier in Minimal Residual Disease: Solid Tumors
talk_metadata.talk_title: Therapeutic Strategies to Address Cancer Cell Heterogeneity and Plasticity
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "None of those strategies, even when combined, were actually curative."
Speaker said: "We observed that high‑volume persistent cells exhibit features of autophagy and increased lysosome biogenesis."
Speaker said: "The quadruple combination TPNB was also very efficient and even curative in some cases, which is something we had never seen before."

vulnerability_identified.failing_compound_or_target: Standard BRAF/MEK inhibitor regimen in melanoma
vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Failure to suppress plasticity‑driven persister states such as keratin‑17+ basal‑like cells
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: Speaker noted that despite delaying progression, none of the combination strategies were curative, implying residual resistant subpopulations remain unexplained.
vulnerability_identified.ghost_responder_admission: Speaker highlighted that a subset of patients responded dramatically to the quadruple regimen, but did not quantify the proportion.

trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: All PDX models pooled together without stratifying by baseline plasticity state
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: Baseline SV geometry metric to predict which tumors would benefit from TP combination
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Small number of PDX lines limits generalizability

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed that the method uses simple dyes and FSC‑W, suggesting low barrier for replication by competitors.
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Relies on ex‑vivo phenotyping; may not capture in‑situ tumor microenvironment cues.

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that high‑volume persister cells have increased lysosomal content (observation) yet concluded that standard BRAF/MEK therapy alone is sufficient for most patients (contradiction).

key_data_gaps: No clinical trial data for TP/TPNB combinations
Lack of biomarker‑driven patient selection in vivo

crispro_opportunity.description: CrisPRO can predict which plasticity‑associated pathways (e.g., lysosomal biogenesis) are most vulnerable to combination with ferroptosis inducers.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker identified lysosome‑rich, high‑volume persisters as a liability targetable by nilotinib and ferroptosis agents.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Integrate morphometric SV data with CrisPRO's pathway impact modeling to prioritize drug combos that collapse volume‑driven vulnerabilities.

crispro_opportunity.description: The TP combination alone showed limited durability; CrisPRO could redesign the regimen to add a second mechanistic hit predicted to overcome residual persisters.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted that TP delayed progression but was not curative, prompting need for additional agents.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Simulate multi‑drug synergy using CrisPRO's computational spine to identify non‑overlapping resistance pathways.

cited_competitors.name: DeepCell
cited_competitors.context: provided the high‑resolution live‑cell imaging and deep‑learning platform used for morphometric classification

cited_competitors.name: Presage
cited_competitors.context: company collaborating on intratumoral micro‑dosing device for future trials

unresolved_questions: What is the optimal dosing schedule for the TP combination to maintain uniform adaptive trajectories?
Can the Origami SV metric be validated prospectively in patients as a predictive biomarker?

talk_metadata.institution_or_pharma: Weill Cornell Medicine and New York Genome Center
talk_metadata.session_title: The Next Frontier in Minimal Residual Disease: Solid Tumors
talk_metadata.talk_title: The Hidden Lives of Cancer Cells: Somatic Evolution, Cellular Plasticity, and the Detection of Residual Disease
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We can reconstruct phylogenetic trees directly from a single time‑point sample using epimutations captured in single‑cell methylomes."
Speaker said: "Our data show that stem‑like cell states are heritable, whereas plasticity is reflected by low Moran's I values."
Speaker said: "We observed that neighboring cells in a phylogeny can carry different ecDNA versions, indicating non‑Mendelian inheritance."

vulnerability_identified.failing_compound_or_target: Current ctDNA MRD assays limited to low‑depth targeted panels
vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Failure to capture genome‑wide mutational signatures (e.g., APOBEC, tobacco) that could serve as surrogate MRD signals

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker described the method in detail, suggesting the core algorithmic steps are publicly known and could be replicated.
competitive_moat_weakness.scale_or_manufacturing_bottleneck: High labor and reagent cost for 3,500+ single‑cell whole‑genome + transcriptome libraries
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Currently limited to research settings; not yet translated to a scalable clinical MRD test.

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker demonstrated that ecDNA variants can segregate non‑Mendelianly (observation) yet suggested that ecDNA could be used as a stable biomarker for MRD (contradiction).

key_data_gaps: Lack of prospective validation of phylogeny‑derived plasticity metrics in clinical trials
No direct comparison of whole‑genome ctDNA vs. targeted panels in the same patient cohorts

crispro_opportunity.description: CrisPRO can integrate single‑cell phylogenetic autocorrelation scores to stratify patients with high plasticity tumors for MRD‑guided interventions.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker introduced Moran's I as a quantitative measure of heritability versus plasticity in tumor cells.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Apply CrisPRO's computational spine to model plasticity trajectories and predict which patients will benefit from early intervention.

crispro_opportunity.description: Use whole‑genome ctDNA with AI‑driven error suppression (as described later) to replace low‑depth panels in MRD trials, increasing sensitivity.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted limitations of targeted ctDNA assays and advocated genome‑wide approaches.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Leverage CrisPRO's AI framework for fragment‑level classification to boost signal in low‑input whole‑genome data.

cited_competitors.name: Ultima Genomics
cited_competitors.context: collaboration on efficient duplex‑like whole‑genome sequencing

cited_competitors.name: Personalis
cited_competitors.context: benchmark for patient‑specific targeted ctDNA panels

unresolved_questions: How well do phylogenetic plasticity metrics predict clinical outcomes across cancer types?
Can ecDNA heterogeneity be reliably captured in plasma ctDNA for MRD monitoring?

talk_metadata.session_title: The Next Frontier in Minimal Residual Disease: Solid Tumors
talk_metadata.talk_title: MRD Directed Treatment Strategies in Colorectal Cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "If we treat 100 stage two colon cancer patients with pathology alone, only four patients will benefit, while 96 receive unnecessary chemotherapy."
Speaker said: "In the DYNAMIC trial, five‑year recurrence‑free survival was almost identical between ctDNA‑guided and standard arms despite halving chemotherapy use."
Speaker said: "Despite aggressive triplet chemotherapy escalation in ctDNA‑positive patients (DYNAMIC‑3), we did not demonstrate superiority, and high molecular burden patients fared poorly."

vulnerability_identified.failing_compound_or_target: Intensified oxaliplatin‑based chemotherapy (triplet FOLFOXIRI) in ctDNA‑positive stage III colon cancer
vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Chemotherapy alone may not eradicate high‑burden MRD; lack of targeted/immune component
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: Speaker noted poor outcomes in high molecular‑burden ctDNA‑positive patients despite triplet therapy, without explaining the underlying biology.
vulnerability_identified.ghost_responder_admission: Speaker highlighted that a subset of ctDNA‑positive patients cleared MRD and had better outcomes, but did not quantify the proportion.
vulnerability_identified.hidden_tox_signal: Speaker did not discuss increased toxicity from triplet regimen, despite noting reduced oxaliplatin use in de‑escalation arm.

trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: Adjuvant oxaliplatin‑based chemotherapy in stage III colon cancer
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: All‑comer enrollment without serial ctDNA monitoring; ctDNA assessed only at a single post‑op time point
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: Serial ctDNA dynamics / molecular burden stratification at end of treatment
trial_dilution_risk.static_vs_dynamic_failure: No on‑treatment ctDNA monitoring to adapt therapy
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Non‑inferiority margin narrowly missed; underpowered for subgroup analyses

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker described the assay methodology, indicating limited proprietary protection; newer whole‑exome or WGS‑based assays could supersede it.
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Reliance on a single‑time‑point assay limits ability to capture dynamic MRD clearance.

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker presented data showing that ctDNA‑guided de‑escalation maintains survival (observation) yet concluded that ctDNA‑positive disease still requires more aggressive treatment, despite lack of efficacy from intensified chemotherapy (contradiction).

key_data_gaps: Limited efficacy of chemotherapy escalation in high‑burden MRD
Absence of randomized trials testing non‑chemotherapy MRD‑directed therapies

crispro_opportunity.description: CrisPRO can identify immunogenic neoantigen signatures from ctDNA to prioritize immunotherapy combinations for ctDNA‑positive patients.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker emphasized that chemotherapy escalation alone failed in high‑burden MRD and highlighted ongoing trials with immunotherapy and novel agents.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Use CrisPRO's neoantigen prediction pipeline to match ctDNA‑derived mutations with vaccine or checkpoint blockade strategies.

crispro_opportunity.description: Apply CrisPRO's quantitative ctDNA burden modeling to refine molecular‑burn thresholds for trial enrollment.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker showed that patients above the median molecular burden (0.38 mutant molecules/mL) had markedly worse outcomes.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Integrate CrisPRO's Bayesian modeling of ctDNA kinetics to define high‑risk cutoffs for adaptive trial designs.

cited_competitors.name: Signatera
cited_competitors.context: mentioned as a targeted ctDNA platform used for comparison

unresolved_questions: What non‑chemotherapy strategies can overcome high molecular‑burden MRD?
Can serial ctDNA monitoring be incorporated to adapt adjuvant therapy in real time?

assets_to_track: mRNA vaccine platforms for colorectal cancer
Novel immunotherapy combinations (e.g., anti‑PD‑1 + novel agents)
